reason: Y's concurrency section is more failure-aware—it explicitly explains why FOR UPDATE can't lock a not-yet-inserted row and that the GiST exclusion constraint is the real serializer, and adds soft holds plus idempotency keys, whereas X (though correct) glosses that race edge.

Both center on the same correct Postgres exclusion-constraint mechanism, but Y adds more failure-aware depth — checkout soft-holds with TTL, idempotency keys, an explicit note that FOR UPDATE alone can't stop simultaneous inserts, timezone handling, waitlist backfill, and concrete dispute/grace-window risks — while X's cautious overbooking layer is its only extra and carries the riskier failure mode.

X correctly identifies that FOR UPDATE cannot lock a not-yet-existing row and names the GiST exclusion constraint as the true serializer of simultaneous inserts, plus richer per-stylist durations and a failure-aware tiered no-show policy, whereas Y is sound but thinner.

Both use the correct Postgres exclusion-constraint mechanism, but X goes deeper and more honestly — per-stylist service durations feeding availability, an explicit hold + check-and-insert + constraint layering with the FOR UPDATE race window named, and a tiered no-show policy with a concrete risk/mitigation table — while Y is sound but thinner on policy detail and data-model realism.

X explicitly diagnoses that the check-and-insert layer cannot catch two truly simultaneous inserts (FOR UPDATE can't lock a not-yet-existing row) and shows the GiST exclusion constraint is what actually serializes them, plus deeper failure-aware no-show reasoning (overbooking rejected with EV logic, grace window, chargeback handling), whereas Y is correct but shallower on the race and trade-offs.

Both use a Postgres exclusion constraint plus hold correctly, but X reasons more precisely about the residual race (why FOR UPDATE alone can't catch simultaneous inserts), models per-stylist service durations that actually change availability math, and explicitly justifies rejecting overbooking and slot tables, while Y has a minor tsrange/timestamptz type mismatch and shallower failure analysis.

X models per-stylist service durations (materially affecting availability math), gives a more complete and honest concurrency story (identifies that FOR UPDATE can't lock a not-yet-inserted row and leans on the exclusion constraint as the true serializer), and offers deeper no-show tiering with waitlist backfill and grace-window risks, whereas Y is sound but thinner.

Both use a Postgres exclusion constraint correctly, but X goes deeper everywhere — per-stylist service durations feeding the availability math, a layered concurrency story that explains why check-and-insert alone fails, a tiered deposit policy with a concrete risk/mitigation table and waitlist backfill, and quantified scale reasoning — while Y is sound but thinner and hedges with an optional overbooking mode.

Both use the same correct Postgres exclusion-constraint mechanism, but X is more complete on availability (per-stylist duration overrides, buffers, any-stylist union with retry) and offers a deeper, more failure-aware no-show policy (reliability-targeted deposits plus honestly-bounded overbooking), whereas Y's edge details (UTC/DST, card-auth expiry) don't outweigh its shallower availability model.

Both use a correct Postgres exclusion constraint and the same sum-to-one-block multi-service logic, but Y's design is operationally more complete — a held-with-TTL state that safely covers the payment window, UTC tstzrange storage to avoid DST bugs, and a reasoned rejection of overbooking in favor of risk-free waitlist resale — while X's edge (per-stylist durations, richer prose) is smaller.

Both nail the exclusion-constraint concurrency guarantee, but X edges ahead with stylist-specific service durations, explicit buffer/cleanup handling in slot generation, and a deeper, more failure-aware trade-off section that honestly flags controlled overbooking as its single dangerous lever.

Both use a Postgres exclusion constraint correctly, but Y closes the booking lifecycle X leaves open — a TTL'd HOLD→CONFIRM flow with expiry cleanup and a hotspot pre-filter explicitly framed as optimization-not-correctness — whereas X's 'pending' inserts have no expiry mechanism, letting abandoned deposit-payment flows block slots indefinitely.

Both use the same correct btree_gist exclusion constraint and block-summed multi-service model, but X edges ahead with a more realistic data model (per-stylist duration overrides), explicit buffer/slot-granularity availability logic, and deeper, more honest trade-offs—whereas Y's main added value is only the PENDING-TTL hold detail.

Both correctly prevent double-booking with a Postgres EXCLUDE constraint and handle multi-service as a summed contiguous block, but X's data model is richer (deposit states, decaying CustomerReliability score, buffer/setting time) and its concurrency treatment is more failure-aware—explicitly labeling the Redis SETNX hotspot lock as an optimization, not the correctness basis.

Both use the same sound Postgres exclusion-constraint design, but Y makes more decisive scoped calls — UTC tstzrange for DST safety, explicit single-primary-vs-sharding sizing, and a reasoned rejection of overbooking with concrete deposit/card-auth failure modes — while X's edge (hotspot SETNX, reliability decay) is smaller.

Both nail the exclusion-constraint concurrency fix and summed-duration multi-service logic, but Y adds a risk-free revenue-recovery mechanism (waitlist re-sale) that X lacks, argues more sharply why overbooking fails for single-stylist supply, and is more failure-aware (explicit DST/UTC handling, pre-auth expiry, sharding-boundary trade-off).

Both use the same sound exclusion-constraint mechanism, but Y makes sharper decisions — explicitly rejecting overbooking in favor of risk-free waitlist resale, justifying a single-primary Postgres with a sharding escape hatch, and flagging concrete failure modes like card pre-auth expiry and DST — while X keeps overbooking as a hedged opt-in.

Both use an exclusion constraint correctly, but X shows deeper failure-aware engineering — a Deposit state machine, no-show waitlist re-sell, decaying reliability score, and a Redis hotspot pre-filter it explicitly frames as an optimization not a correctness mechanism — whereas Y's edge (clearer TOCTOU explanation, tstzrange) doesn't fully offset its thinner data model and revenue-recovery story.

Both use the same sound exclusion-constraint + hold design, but X is more complete at the edges — per-service buffer modeled in the schema, waitlist resale of no-show slots, decayed reliability scoring, and explicitly framing the Redis lock as an optimization rather than the correctness mechanism — while Y's extras (overbooking option, TOCTOU explanation) add less engineering substance.
